coredata.dc:title: Targeted and imaging-guided chemo-photothermal ablation achieved by combining upconversion nanoparticles and protein-capped gold nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chen
Xue, Ruibin
Gulzar, Arif
Kuang, Ye
Shao, Hua
Gai, Shili
Yang, Dan
He, Fei
Yang, Piaoping
coredata.dc:description: For precise targeting therapy, nanocarriers are typically employed to realize tumor-guided delivery of chemotherapeutic drugs, which should ideally kill cancer cell without side effect under controlled release. Besides, as the temperature is fatal for photothermal therapy (PTT), enhancing photothermal conversion efficiency is essential for improving PTT efficacy. Moreover, as PTT is a localized therapy technique, maintaining high penetration depth and simultaneously achieving multimodal imaging during theranostics are conducive to clinical application. Aiming at resolving these plaguing challenges, here we developed a novel anti-tumor platform by combining upconversion nanoparticles with protein-capped gold nanodots through a facile route, following by loading chemotherapeutic drug (doxorubicin, DOX) and conjugating targeting agent (folic acid, FA). Such nanoplatform with luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET) effect showed enhanced PTT efficacy under near-infrared (NIR) laser irradiation, and FA conjugation is capable of further targeting cancer cells, namely the focus location in vivo. Meanwhile, the nanocarrier with pH-sensitive DOX release ability would contribute to intensive release responding to acidic tumor microenvironment assisted by NIR light excitation induced heat effect, further improving in vivo anti-tumor efficiency. Additionally, by combining our upconversion/Au nanohybrid with multimodal imaging, direct cancer cell killing responses could be obtained at a deeper penetration depth, promising for completely inhibiting tumor proliferation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Upconversion
DOX
Multimodal imaging
Targeted photothermal therapy
BSA

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GdOF:Yb/Er@(GNDs@BSA) nanocomposite conjugated with DOX and FA
In vitro biocompatibility of GGBDFs evaluation
Photothermal evaluation of GGBDFs sample solution
Controlled release validation of DOX from GGBDFs
In vitro hemolysis assay of GGBDFs
Photothermal (PT), computed tomography (CT), and magnetic resonance (MR) detection
In vitro UCL, photothermal and biocompatibility of GGBDFs
DOX release, cytotoxicity, hemocompatibility of GGBDFs
In vivo multimodal imaging and anti-cancer evaluation of GGBDFs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) TEM images, magnified TEM images, and corresponding size distributions of Gd(OH)x(CO3)yFz:Yb3+/Er3+ (a), GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+ (b), and GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA) (c). (d) High-magnified TEM image of GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA), inset is the corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image. (e) X–ray diffraction patterns, (f) XPS survey spectrum and (g) FT–IR spectra of corresponding samples.

(a) Digital photographs of different volumes of GNDs condensation after diluted with 95 mL DI water. (b) Upconversion emission spectra of GdOF:Yb/Er@(GNDs@BSA) with different volumes of GNDs solutions and UV–vis absorption spectra of GNDs and GNDs@BSA. (c) The red/green ratio with different volumes of GNDs solution added. (d) CIE chromaticity diagram changes with different volumes of GNDs solution added. (e) Schematic energy transfer process between UCNPs with GNDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

CLSM images of HeLa cells cultured with GGBDFs for 0.5 h, 1 h, and 3 h. (The blue fluorescence emission represents the cell uptake of DAPI and the red fluorescence emission represents the cell uptake of DOX). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) In vitro UCL imaging of GGBDFs in HeLa cells under NIR irradiation. (b) The photothermal response evaluation of GGBDFs at varying concentration under NIR irradiation. (c) The photothermal conversion cycling performance of GGBDFs under NIR irradiation. (d) Warming and cooling curves and (e) photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of GGBDFs with different amount of GNDs added under NIR irradiation. (f) The NIR thermal imaging comparison between DI water and GGBDFs solution under NIR irradiation. (980 nm NIR laser with power of 0.5 W cm−2 is selected as the irradiation source).
Effect of pH (a) and NIR irradiation (b) on DOX release from GGBDFs.

(a) CLSM images of HeLa cancer cells stained by calcein-AM/PI which were incubated with medium as control and GGBDFs sample under 980 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W cm−2, 10 min). In vitro cytotoxicity of Control, GGBDs, GGBFs, GGBDFs without (b) or with (c) 980 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W cm−2, 10 min).

(a) NIR thermal images of the mice injected with saline and GGBDFs (25 mg kg−1) at U14 tumor-bearing site with 980 nm laser irradiation (0.8 W cm−2) for 10 min. In vitro CT (b) images and (c) values of GGBDFs versus Gd concentration. (d) In vivo CT images comparison between saline and GGBDFs in U14 tumor bearing mice with i.v. injection. In vitro (e) T 1-weighted MR images and (f) 1/T 1 values of GGBDFs versus Gd concentration. (g) In vivo T 1-weighted MR images comparison at U14 tumor-bearing site before and after GGBDFs i.v. injection. In vitro (h) T 2-weighted MR images and (i) 1/T 2 values of GGBDFs versus Gd concentration. (j) In vivo T 2-weighted MR images comparison at U14 tumor-bearing site before and after GGBDFs i.v. injection.

The body weights (a), tumor volumes (b), representative euthanized photographs (c), tumors (d), and H&E stained slices (e) of U14 tumor-bearing mice with 14 days treatments in different groups. (0.8 W cm−2 980 nm NIR laser is selected as the irradiation source).
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA)–DOX–FA.

head.ce:title: Targeted and imaging-guided chemo-photothermal ablation achieved by combining upconversion nanoparticles and protein-capped gold nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology
College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Technology, Jilin Institute of Chemical Technology, Jinlin 132022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Under NIR laser irradiation, such upconversion/Au nanoplatform with luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET) effect showed enhanced PTT efficacy and multimodal imaging.

For precise targeting therapy, nanocarriers are typically employed to realize tumor-guided delivery of chemotherapeutic drugs, which should ideally kill cancer cell without side effect under controlled release. Besides, as the temperature is fatal for photothermal therapy (PTT), enhancing photothermal conversion efficiency is essential for improving PTT efficacy. Moreover, as PTT is a localized therapy technique, maintaining high penetration depth and simultaneously achieving multimodal imaging during theranostics are conducive to clinical application. Aiming at resolving these plaguing challenges, here we developed a novel anti-tumor platform by combining upconversion nanoparticles with protein-capped gold nanodots through a facile route, following by loading chemotherapeutic drug (doxorubicin, DOX) and conjugating targeting agent (folic acid, FA). Such nanoplatform with luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET) effect showed enhanced PTT efficacy under near-infrared (NIR) laser irradiation, and FA conjugation is capable of further targeting cancer cells, namely the focus location in vivo. Meanwhile, the nanocarrier with pH-sensitive DOX release ability would contribute to intensive release responding to acidic tumor microenvironment assisted by NIR light excitation induced heat effect, further improving in vivo anti-tumor efficiency. Additionally, by combining our upconversion/Au nanohybrid with multimodal imaging, direct cancer cell killing responses could be obtained at a deeper penetration depth, promising for completely inhibiting tumor proliferation.

ce:section.ce:para: As a massive mortal disease, cancer has become a major public health concern, which is still paid considerable attention to find new approaches to combat [1]. Thus, numerous nanomedicines especially novel nanomaterials have been developed to provide unprecedented opportunities for the promotion of cancer diagnosis and therapy [2,3]. Inspired by uncomplicated synthesis methods, low/non-toxicity, good solubility, excellent biocompatibility, negligible photobleaching, and protein-based gold nanocrystals were explored for extensive applications in biomedicine and bioimaging [4–6]. Among them, bovine serum albumin (BSA) capped gold nanodots (GNDs) have been popularly employed as contrast agent and photothermal agent [7–9]. As a common protein template, BSA is often used to conjugate targeted cancer agents which could be adhered to the surface of BSA [10,11]. Typically, folic acid (FA) could modify the Au@BSA nanocomposite, exhibiting low toxicity and significant tumor targeting efficiency in vitro and in vivo owing to the mediation of folate receptor [12,13]. Besides, functionalized chemotherapeutic or fluorescent agents coupled free amino groups of BSA enable diversification of the nanoplateforms, which can further enhance the treatment efficiency and extend diagnostic measures [14]. Doxorubicin (DOX) attached to the BSA layer has the characteristics of pH-responsive release, ensuring that the drug loading nanocomposite is smothered in the physiological environment (pH ∼ 7.4), while gradually released in tumor acidic tissues (pH < 7.4) [15,16]. This barrier release mode can minimize the attack of drugs on normal tissues and reduce the side effect during transportation [17]. Moreover, thanks to either the intrinsic properties or the ancillary ligands, GNDs possess good chemical and biological stability, which not only generates photothermal effect for cancer therapy, but also contributes to the synergistic chemotherapy of DOX [18,19]. Therefore, BSA-based gold nanodots are very conducive to drug delivery and targeted theranostics.

Although this nanohybrid has a significant advantage in anti-tumor therapy, there are still several problems perplexing throughout the examination and treatment. Firstly, due to their small size and low aspect ratio, GNDs@BSA nanoparticles have a tendency of accumulation in the EPR (enhanced permeability and retention) organs (e.g., liver, pancreas, and spleen) [20–23]. In long-term treatments, the uncertain side effects will be imperceptibly induced by the residual noble metal in vivo [24]. In addition, the emblematical surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band of GNDs@BSA is around 580 nm, which is located in visible region rather than near-infrared (NIR) region [25]. Because of a low penetration provided by short-wavelength absorption, gold particles are merely suitable for superficial soft tissues or in vitro treatment, resulting in severe inhibitions during anti-tumor therapy [26–28]. Furthermore, the single gold nanostructure is difficult to meet the requirement of multimodal imaging in cancer diagnosis. Routine examination methods such as nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and fluorescence are unable to be realized by Au nanoparticles, which do not benefit for the flexible and optional detection in tumor sites [29–31]. Hence, it is strongly desirable to develop a new theranostics nanoplatform to fix the defects related above.

Recently, lanthanide (Ln)-doped upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) are particularly appealing because of their tremendous potential in laser components, stereo displays, new breed light source, information identification, NIR counters, low-background bioimaging, and solar energy conversion [32–40]. So far, as one of the most appropriate host materials, hexagonal phase GdOF is able to upconvert the NIR light to UV–vis light via Ln3+ ion doping [41–43]. On the one hand, the intrinsic NMR effect of gadolinium ions provides a new approach for multimodal imaging [44]. Compared with computed tomography (CT) method, NMR imaging is much less harmful and more comfortable to the patients during in vivo diagnosis [45]. On the other hand, rare-earth co-doped such as Yb3+ and Er3+ can offer evident upconversion fluorescence emission [46,47]. Yb3+ absorbs the NIR excitation photon and then Er3+ emits upconversion luminescence (UCL), which could be applied to fluorescence labeling and luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET) [48,49]. Additionally, good biocompatibility of UCNPs guarantees the application of biomedicine in vivo, especially in drug delivery and metabolism of organs [50,51]. Considering the complementary advantage of UCNPs, a variety of gold nanocrystals are combined with them to form new nanocomposite for cancer theranostics [52,53]. However, Au/UCNPs nanocomposites with targeted, low toxicity and multimodal imaging for chemo-photothermal therapy are still being explored.

In this study, GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs were wrapped in abundant of GNDs@BSA, then successively modified with DOX and FA to build GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA)–DOX–FA nanohybrids. As an energy donor, GdOF host doped with Yb3+/Er3+ ions was used to construct an LRET system for SPR band absorption of GNDs and generate the photothermal effect. Intelligently, UCNPs dominated their excellent luminescence property and NIR conversion, while GNDs@BSA offered their brilliant X-ray attenuation property and photothermal ablations. Moreover, BSA as a free amino group template conjugated with chemotherapeutics DOX can significantly improve the efficiency of anti-cancer therapy, especially in the NIR laser induced heating control. After loading identification of FA, specific binding to the abnormal folate receptor (FR)-overexpression in U14 tumor cells will occur, focusing on the chemo-photothermal therapy site and alleviating the concussion at normal part. Besides, the BSA and FA coating replaced toxic PVP surfactant to further improve the biocompatibility and provide a safety guarantee for in vivo cancer theranostics.

Gd2O3 (99.99%), Yb2O3 (99.99%), Er2O3 (99.99%), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O, 99.99%), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium fluoride (KF), urea (99.7%) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Concentrated nitric acid (HNO3, 98%), n-cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 99%) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were achieved from Beijing Chemical Corporation. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, M.W. 30,000), 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC), glutaraldehyde, calcein AM, propidium iodide (PI), 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), cis-aconitic anhydride (98%), doxorubicin (DOX) and folic acid (FA) were acquired from Aldrich.

Briefly, rare earth nitrate, deionized (DI) water and ultrapure water were firstly prepared by our similar working methods [54]. Then, 1 mL 0.78 M of Gd(NO3)3, 1 mL 0.20 M of Yb(NO3)3, 1 mL 0.02 M of Er(NO3)3, 1 mL, 0.1 mM of KF, 3 g urea and 0.15 g CTAB were mixed with 45 mL DI water in a 250 mL beaker for 10 min. Followed by all the chemical reagent dissolved, the glass beaker was placed in a water bath for at least 3 h. When the solution temperature was cooled down, the precipitate product was achieved by a constant centrifugation (4600 rpm, 5 min) and repeated washing with DI water/ethanol in turn. After a continuous drying, Gd(OH)x(CO3)yFz:Yb/Er precursors were calcined in a crucible at 700 °C for 200 min. Finally, GdOF:Yb/Er nanoparticles were directly obtained without grinding and corrosion.

PVP-capped gold nanodots (GNDs) were prepared with a certain modification based on a conventional method given by Liu et al [55]. Typically, 10 mL 2.5 mM of HAuCl4 solution, 140 mL DI water, and 0.14 g PVP were mixed in a flask for 30 min. Then, 5 mL 0.1 M of NaBH4 solution (with ice-cold treatment) was injected under vigorous stirring and the mixture changed from colorless to red in the next few minutes. The as-prepared solution was concentrated ten-fold by high speed centrifugation (13,500 rpm, 10 min) for further use.

BSA conjugation was based on an effective method offered by Moustafa et al [56]. Firstly, BSA powder and PBS solution were mixed in a conical flask, then shaken in an orbital incubator overnight at a concentration of 0.25 mM. Afterwards, 1 mL BSA and 1 mL GNDs solution were vigorously stirred for 10 h at 27 °C. The GNDs@BSA aqueous solution was achieved by a centrifugation (12,000 rpm, 10 min) and stored in ultrapure water at room temperature.

Firstly, 1.8 g GdOF:Yb/Er NPs were dissolved in 20 mL 1 M of NaOH solution. After 8 h of stirring and 8 h drying at 60 °C, the as-treated GdOF:Yb/Er NPs were diluted with 70 mL DI water and then 20 mL ATPES was added. Followed by stirred at 55 °C for 8 h, the product was collected by centrifugation and washing three times to extract free ATPES. Then, GNDs@BSA condensation (0 mL, 1 mL, 2 mL, 3 mL, 4 mL and 5 mL) was diluted into 90 mL DI water and as-obtained GdOF:Yb/Er–ATPES was re-added in the mixture. After another 8 h stirring at room temperature, GdOF:Yb/Er@(GNDs@BSA) nanocomposites were obtained by centrifugation and washing for several times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GdOF:Yb/Er@(GNDs@BSA) nanocomposite conjugated with DOX and FA
ce:section.ce:para: DOX and FA functionalization was performed on the surface of GdOF:Yb/Er@(GNDs@BSA) nanocomposites via NHS and EDC conjugation according to the previous literature [57]. Typically, 1 mg as-prepared cis-DOX (cis-aconitic anhydride and DOX were mixed with the mass percentage of 3:7), 1 mg FA, 5 mg EDC and 2 mg NHS were diluted into 20 mL DI water and then mixed in light-free conditions for 4 h. Subsequently, 50 mg GdOF:Yb/Er@(GNDs@BSA) nanocomposites were added in the solution and kept stirring for another 12 h. Finally, free DOX and FA were separated from the GdOF:Yb/Er@(GNDs@BSA)-DOX-FA products via centrifugation and washing three times (the final products defined as GGBDFs).

Morphology was performed on a transmission electron microscope (TEM, H-600, Hitachi) at a 200 kV of accelerating voltage. Phase analysis were made by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D/max TTR-III, Rigaku) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15,405 nm). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were determined by a VG ESCALAB MK II electron spectrometer using Mg Kα excitation at 1200 eV. Infrared spectra were obtained by Flourier transformation infrared spectrophotometer (FT-IR, F360, AVATAR). Zeta potential was achieved on a zetasizer (1000HSA, Malvern). Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra were detected on a UV–vis spectrometer (U1901, PERSEE). UCL emission spectra were achieved using a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS 980, Edinburgh) with a 980 nm laser irradiation device. Photothermal performance was evaluated by an infrared camera (R300SR-HD, NEC). In vitro UCL images were observed using an inverted fluorescence microscopy (Ti-S, Nikon) with a 980 nm NIR laser beam. Cellular and pathological observation was gained using a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Leica SP8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biocompatibility of GGBDFs evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: To test the in vitro biocompatibility of GGBDFs, the standard cell assay of MTT was carried out on the L929 fibroblast cells. Briefly, the cell line L929 was incubated in 96-well plates at ∼8000 cells well−1 followed by the GGBDFs media solutions with different concentrations (15.63, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, 1000 μg mL−1) were seeded into each array of the well plates. After incubation in 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h, as-prepared MTT solution was moved to 96-well plates (20 μL well−1) and then cultured for another 4 h. Subsequently, the supernatant in wells was dislodged and DMSO (150 μL well−1) was moved into each well. After 10 min gently shaking, the plates were embedded in a microtiter plate reader (BioTek ELX 800) and examined at the wavelength of 490 nm. Besides, the in vitro biocompatibility of GNDs and UCNPs assays were also performed on with the similar procedure of standard MTT.

To estimate the uptake behavior of GGBDFs nanohybrid, HeLa cells were selected and then incubated in 6 well plates. After incubation at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h, cells were cultured on the slide with a density of ∼5 × 104 cells well−1. With a varying incubation time, 2 mL 0.6 mg mL−1 of GGBDFs media solution was injected to each well. Then, all slides were extracted to wash and dried until the free GGBDFs missed. Afterwards, the cells on the slides were fixed by glutaraldehyde and nucleus labeled by DAPI solution (1 mL 20 mg mL−1). Finally, the images of cell coupled with GGBDFs sample were achieved by CLSM. Moreover, after 48 h exposed in air, all cells were collected to detect the concentration of Au by ICP–MS.

Firstly, the HeLa cells incubation of in vitro UCL imaging observation is alike to the method of in vitro cell uptake test. Thereafter, GGBDFs samples with different amount of GNDs (0 mL or 3 mL) were cultured in 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 1 h, cells were successively washed, dried and fixed according to the standard operation related above. Finally, the UCL images of GGBDFs in HeLa cancer cells were captured by inverted fluorescence microscopy attached NIR excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal evaluation of GGBDFs sample solution
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 200 μL aqueous solution of GGBDFs with different concentrations (100, 25, 12.5, 6.25 and 0 ppm) was moved to the relative well in 96-well plate. Then, 0.5 W cm−2 of 980 nm NIR laser emitter was erected above the liquid level. After 10 min of NIR laser irradiation and undisturbed cooling to the environment temperature, the temperature and infrared image of the samples are captured and tracked by the infrared camera (R300SR-HD, NEC). The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) was fitted and reckoned as previous work, which is given in Table S1 [58,59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Controlled release validation of DOX from GGBDFs
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro release performance of DOX from GGBDFs was evaluated by varying the pH value of the solutions. Typically, 1 mL 50 mg mL−1 of GGBDFs with 30 μg of DOX loaded was first located in a dialysis tube. Afterwards, they were embedded in plastic tubes which contain saline-sodium citrate (SSC) and NaOH solution. It is worth noting that pH values were fixed at 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0 and 10.0, respectively. After the channel was well installed, all tubes were consecutively shaken, and the medium was substituted with fresh medium at anticipated time. Meanwhile, the quantitative analysis of DOX release was carried out by using UV–vis spectrometry.

We first intuitively observed the inactivation rate of HeLa cells by AM/PI staining. Similar to the incubation method in cellular uptake and in vitro UCL imaging, HeLa cancer cells were cultured on four slides with a density of ∼5 × 104 cells well−1. After incubated with/without GGBDFs samples (2 mL 0.6 mg mL−1) for another 4 h, cells on the slides were dried, fixed and strained with Calcein-AM/PI, then observed and checked by CLSM. Furthermore, to examine the in vitro cytotoxicity of GGBDFs against HeLa cancer cells, standard MTT assay was also employed as the flow of in vitro biocompatibility test. Briefly, after incubation in 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h, HeLa cells were incubated with samples (15.63, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, 1000 μg mL−1) for another 24 h. Whereafter, each well was excited by 980 nm laser (0.5 W cm−2) for 10 min. After incubated for 24 h, the cells were performed on the standard MTT assay with the same procedure explained above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro hemolysis assay of GGBDFs
ce:section.ce:para:
The in vitro hematotoxicity of GGBDFs was carried out by employing human red blood cells, which is offered by the Hospital of Harbin Engineering University. Via anticoagulation, collection, rinse, condense, and purification, human red blood cell suspension (1.2 mL) were blended with PBS solution of GGBDFs (0.3 mL; 25, 50, 100, 200, 400, and 800 mg mL−1). After standing 37 °C for 4 h, the supernatant was separated by centrifugation and then examined at 541 nm. The hemolysis percentage (HSP) was reckoned as the following equation:
HSP
%
=
PG - PC
-
/
PC
+
- - PC
-
where PG, PC(–), and PC(+) are the absorbance of the GGBDFs samples, negative control and positive control, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal (PT), computed tomography (CT), and magnetic resonance (MR) detection
ce:section.ce:para: For in vitro CT evaluation, the GGBDFs samples were first dissolved in PBS solution with the given concentrations (0–25 mg mL−1). After that, the mixture was respectively injected in the plastic tubes which arranged in a row. Subsequently, the GGBDFs samples were settled in 120 kV CT scanner (64-slice, Philips) to obtain the data and images. Similarly, MR samples of GGBDFs were detected by 0.5 T MRI magnet (NM120-Analyst, Shanghai Niumai Co., Ltd) with the varying concentrations from 0 to 1.0 mg mL−1. Furthermore, in vivo PT, CT, and MR bioimages were gained by removing the tube with mice. Typically, the tumor-bearing mice with i.v. pre-injection of GGBDFs were anesthetized with 2% isoflurane during photo shooting. Followed by the mice fixed on the plastic plate, images were captured by the apparatus of PT, CT, and MR, respectively.

Typically, female Balb/c mice (∼21.5 g) were brought from the Second Affiliated Hospital of Harbin Medical University (Harbin, China). Afterwards, 100 μL of U14 cells (∼5.5 × 107 mL−1) were injected in the left axilla mice to induce the tumor-bearing. After two days of proliferation, the volume of tumors arrived at ∼200 mm3 to complete the tumor model. In order to explore the in vivo distribution of GGBDFs, twenty-eight tumor-bearing mice with GGBDFs (25 mg kg−1) i.v. injection treatment were randomly divided into seven groups (N = 4). After 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 12 h, 1 day, 3 days, and 5 days maintenance, five major organs (heart, kidney, liver, lung, and spleen) were kept in glass tubes, respectively. Followed by weighted, the organs were eliminated by aqua regia and hydrogen peroxide, then the concentration of Au in the as-prepared aqueous solution was analyzed by ICP–MS. For in vivo evaluation of chemo-photothermal therapy, thirty tumor-bearing mice were classified as five groups (N = 6). Each group has different treatment method (group 1: PBS as control, group 2: GGBDF without NIR irradiation, group 3: GGBDs with NIR irradiation, group 4: GGBF with NIR irradiation, group 5: GGBDFs with NIR irradiation), which the tumor-site was performed on i.v. injection (120 μL, 2 mg mL−1) and 0.8 W cm−2 980 nm laser irradiation for 10 min for each two days. Meanwhile, the tumor size and body weight of each mouse were counted. Followed by 14 days therapy, the emblematical mice for each group were euthanatized to acquire main organs (heart, kidney, liver, lung, and spleen) and tumors, which were carefully dehydrated, sealed and sliced on the slides. After stained with hematoxylin and eosin, tissues were observed by CLSM. Besides, the survival rate of mice in long-term therapy (8 weeks) was also recorded.

The primary procedure for the synthetic evolution of GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA)–DOX–FA nanohybrid is presented in Scheme 1 . First of all, Gd(OH)x(CO3)yFz:Yb/Er precursors were synthesized by co-precipitation method, then calcined into GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+ nanoparticles by muffle furnace. Meanwhile, colloidal GNDs with a diameter below 4 nm were synthesized by a one-pot growth method with a little modification. Afterwards, PVP on the surface of GNDs were replaced by BSA after vigorous stirring overnight. Followed by etching of NaOH and modification of amino-propyltriethoxysilane (APTES), GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs are conjugated with amino groups on the surface. Yielding GNDs-decorated UCNPs, GNDs@BSA were adsorbed on the surface of GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+. Finally, DOX and FA were successively conjugated on the surface of GdOF:Yb/Er@(GNDs@BSA) to construct a multifunctional theranostic nanoplatform for both targeted PTT and chemotherapy.

The Gd(OH)x(CO3)yFz:Yb3+/Er3+ precursor, GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs and GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA) were carefully characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, which have well-defined spherical shapes with an average diameters of ∼33, ∼28 and ∼47 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 a–c). In Fig. 1d, several composited phases of GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA) were presented and confirmed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and the corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) images. Moreover, local elemental mapping and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis of GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA) revealed the uniform distribution of Gd, Yb, F, O and Au elements, demonstrating successful GNDs@BSA attachment on the UCNPs (Figs. S1 and S2). Besides, GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+ and Au characteristic diffraction peaks showed up in the X-ray patterns of those nanoparticles, indirectly evidencing GdOF and GNDs co-exist in one nanostructure (Fig. 1e). In addition, peaks exhibited around 88 eV, 142 eV, 199 eV, 531 eV and 684 eV by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra verified Au, Gd, Yb, O and F located in such GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA) samples (Fig. 1f). As shown in Fig. S3, the zeta potential of Gd(OH)x(CO3)yFz:Yb3+/Er3+ and GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+ almost kept at around 0 mV, while the zeta potential of GNDs changed from +26.53 mV to −26.80 mV after BSA coating. As a contrast, GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA) displayed an obvious negative potential relative to GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+, identifying GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+ was layered by GNDs@BSA. Moreover, the desired conjugation of BSA, DOX, and FA on nanohybrids can be confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FT–IR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1g) [60]. The absorption bands of –OH, O–C–O, π-CO3 2– and δ-CO3 2– can be apparently attributed to 3345 and 1082, 1523 and 1401, 843, 753 and 688 cm−1, respectively, which is similar to the previous works. The typical characteristic peaks of BSA (613, 1540 and 1655 cm−1), DOX (1064–1270 cm−1) and FA (956 and 1063 cm−1) are also observed, confirming the smooth BSA coating, DOX loading and FA anchoring [57]. All the results illustrate that the GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA)–DOX–FA nanohybrid was undoubtedly received as envisaged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro UCL, photothermal and biocompatibility of GGBDFs
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the effect of GNDs on upconversion luminescence (UCL) and photothermal performance, samples with different amount of GNDs solution were synthesized by the same method. As shown in Fig. 2 a, with GNDs volume increased, the color of the mixture transferred from colorless to dark, suggesting that the absorbance of mixtures were indeed due to the extinction of GNDs in visible region. After coating with BSA, the UV–vis absorption band of GNDs was slightly red shifted, overlapping the green and red emissions of GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs. For this reason, the fluorescence intensity of GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA) was apparently decreased with the amount of GNDs increasing (Fig. 2b). Interestingly, the emission decay of 661 nm (4F9/2 → 4I15/2) in the red region is much heavier than that of 520 nm (2H11/2 → 4I15/2) and 540 nm (4S3/2 → 4I15/2) in the green region, which is not consistent with the UV–vis absorption of GNDs, because the red emission of Er3+ is more inclined to be absorbed by GNDs, thereby conducing to improve the photothermal efficiency of the nanocomposites. As displayed in Fig. 2c, the ratio decline of red to green kept step with the attenuation of emission intensity, illustrating that GNDs scattering dominates the fluorescence decay. Although GNDs coating decreases the UCL emission intensity of UCNPs, the fluorescence color of nanohybrids also becomes continuously tunable (Fig. 2d), which provide a new way for polychromatic fluorescence imaging and GNDs release monitoring in vivo. The possible energy transfer process and photofluorescence-thermal mechanism of GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA) nanohybrid were sketchily defined in Fig. 2e. First, energy transfer from the 2F7/2 → 2F5/2 levels due to the Yb3+ ions of UCNP cores possess a strong absorption at ∼980 nm. Followed by Er3+ ions absorb the photons pumped from Yb3+ ions, emissions at 520 nm (green light region), 540 nm (green light region), and 661 nm (red light region) are excited from 2H11/2 → 4I15/2, 4S3/2 → 4I15/2 and 4F9/ → 4I15/2 transitions, respectively. Both the green and red light emissions can be directly used in bioimaging with low background fluorescence noise. However, partly fluorescence emissions are able to be converted to GNDs@BSA by LRET effect, depending on the closer distance and the spectra overlapping between UCNPs and noble metals. GNDs not only absorb the energy from UCNPs to generate heat but also scatter the NIR incident light, which contributes to fluorescence decay. Thus, the amount of GND needs to be controlled and coordinated to balance the efficiency between light and heat, benefiting for the photochemical stability and synergetic cancer theranostics.

Meanwhile, benefiting from 980 nm NIR laser irradiation and LRET effect, the photothermal conversion property of GGBDFs was further evaluated. In Fig. 4b, under 980 nm NIR irradiation, the concentration-dependent photothermal response of GGBDFs was obviously presented, while the temperatures of GNDs and DI water have little variation with time goes on. Furthermore, after three cycles of heating and cooling (10 min heating coupled with 12 min cooling for each cycle), the photothermal activity of sample aqueous solution was maintained (Fig. 4c), suggesting the good chemical stability and photothermal performance of GGBDFs. For fear of the side effects triggered by overheating, the photothermal conversion efficiency of GGBDFs with different GNDs volume was also estimated and calculated (Figs. 4d, S6 and Table S1). In comparison with other samples, GGBDFs with 4 mL GNDs have a higher photothermal conversion efficiency (36.2%), which can be objectively inspected by NIR thermal imaging (Fig. 4e,f). Accordingly, combined with the analysis of fluorescence properties, GGBDFs with 4 mL GNDs were selected as a popular candidate for the following assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX release, cytotoxicity, hemocompatibility of GGBDFs
ce:section.ce:para: Since the pH-sensitive cis-aconityl linker was functionalized on the surface of GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA) to conjugate DOX, quantitative analysis of in vitro drug release was carried out in different pH media. As displayed in Fig. 5 a, with the pH value gradually decreasing, the release rate of DOX increased rapidly, illustrating that DOX of GGBDFs was preferred to release in the acid environment. In comparison, the release amount of DOX at pH 5.0 is 4.2 ∼ 4.8-fold higher than most alkaline conditions, which is contribute to the acid ambient broken cis-aconityl linkage [12]. Furthermore, since the heat generated from GNDs under NIR irradiation improving the release of DOX, drug release profiles were examined under 37 °C and pH = 7.4. In Fig. 5b, it was found that a significant release of DOX is in the presence of NIR irradiation, which allows the DOX release to reach by 50% in 24 h. Therefore, NIR photothermal induced release character is favorable to acquire better DOX release during anti-cancer treatment.

Based on the high photothermal efficiency of GGBDFs, in vitro cytotoxicity was tested on HeLa cells. First, in vitro anti-cancer ability of GGBDFs was intuitively and vividly examined by AM–propidium iodide (PI) assays. As observed by CLSM images, the red region from GGBDFs with 980 nm NIR irradiation group was apparently larger than control group (Fig. 6 a), exhibiting a better cell uptake and in vitro photothermal killing ability after incubation with HeLa cells for 24 h. Subsequently, the potency of GGBDFs for HeLa cancer-cell killing was measured on four groups including without samples added, with GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA)–FA added, with GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA)–DOX added, with GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA)–DOX–FA added (hereinafter abbreviated to Control, GGBFs, GGBDs, and GGBDFs, respectively). As a contrast, high viability (95%–100%) was received in all four groups without any light treatment (Fig. 6b). However, there exists significant cytotoxicity difference among the four groups after 980 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 6c). The IC50 values are 361.1, 162.0 and 10.6 μg L–1 for the GGBFs, GGBDs, GGBDFs groups, respectively. All these results inferred that FA conjugation remarkably improves the cell uptake ability, connecting cell membranes and GGBDFs upon targeting molecules.

Before further exploring the in vivo bioimaging and anti-tumor capacity of GGBDFs, the hemocompatibility was prior assayed on human red blood cells. GGBDFs present little hemolytic activity (<2.5%) even though the concentration reached 1000 μg mL−1 (Fig. S7). Hemolysis results corroborate their good hemocompatibility and biocompatibility, indicating the smooth biological circulating of blood cells in vivo. Meanwhile, the in vivo biocompatibility of GGBDFs was also investigated. According to the regulation promulgated by National of China for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals and Harbin Science and Technology Bureau, necessary care was given to all mice during the treatment. For security reasons, pathomorphology observation of five main organs (heart, kidney, liver, lung, and spleen) was employed as a testify (Fig. S8). As expected, there was no obvious distinction between GGBDFs and control group, which set the stage for the following in vivo theranostics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo multimodal imaging and anti-cancer evaluation of GGBDFs
ce:section.ce:para: Since GGBDFs have a considerable temperature increment, in vivo NIR thermal images were received and caught by the infrared thermal camera. After irradiation under 0.8 W cm−2 for 15 min, the temperature at the tumor-bearing site reached by 58.6 °C, which shows the color change from blue to red in the central region of tumor (Fig. 7 a and S9). However, the temperature of PBS and GNDs were almost settled, and the color also stayed blue. For supplementary means, CT and MR imaging were further carried out by employing GGBDFs as a contrast agent. With the advantage of high resolution and deep tissue penetration, CT was generally applied in bioassay and bioimaging. Compared with the previous work and commercial Iopromide, GGBDFs possess an enhanced CT signal with a slope of 25.99 ± 0.904 (R 2 = 0.995) (Fig. 7b,c) [60,61]. Furthermore, accompanied by the CT value started from 65.5 HU to 1458.1 HU, in vivo CT images were clearly presented at both bony and soft tissue windows after intravenous injection of GGBDFs (Fig. 7d). Besides, MR with more biosafety guarantees motivates us for the application in excellent bioimaging. The r 1 longitudinal relaxivity and r 2 transverse relaxivity of GGBDFs were derived from a gadolinium concentration-dependent linear fit, which has advantage over the common agent Gd-GDTA (r 1 = 7.50 ± 0.113 mm−1 s−1, r 2 = 6.52 ± 0.089 mm−1 s−1) (Fig. 7e, f, h, i) [62]. Moreover, in vivo MR imaging technique was also employed on the tumor-bearing mice. As displayed in Fig. 7g,j, there was a significant eradiate signals difference recognized in the tumor site between GGBDFs and control group. All these analyses indicate that GGBDFs have excellent bioimaging and targeting ability for a potential contrast agent.

In this in vivo experiment, U14 tumor cell lines were inoculated on the left axillary for each mouse. Followed by this, the biodistribution of GGBDFs in tumor-bearing mice was measured by ICP–MS, confirming that the peak accumulation of Au in tumor sites was at ∼ 12 h after i.v. injection (Fig. S10). On the basis of this revelation, we ascertained the optimum laser irradiation time of GGBDFs during the tumor-inhibition. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the fluctuation of body weight in all five groups has no significant distinction in 14 d anti-tumor treatment, implying the good in vivo biocompatibility of GGBDFs. Distinguished from the control and GGBDFs group, definite therapy efficiency emerged in the other three groups (Fig. 8b–d). Interestingly, GGBDFs coupled with NIR laser irradiation inhibits tumor most efficiency among all test group because of the tumor targeting and chemotherapy with the photothermal promotion. In addition, the remarkable apoptosis of cancer cells presented in GGBDFs with NIR irradiation group assisted the results of tumor size with histopathology method (Fig. 8e). As a famous photothermal material, noble metal has been well reported for PTT. For example, Pan’s group designed well-formed Pt@Fe2O3 nanorods, Au@Pt nanodendrites and PtRu bimetallic nanocomplexes as novel theranostic agents [63–65]. And all the theranostic agents provide outstanding PTT effect which is consistent with our results. Besides, compared with other groups, mice injected GGBDFs with NIR irradiation group survived for more than 8 weeks, illustrating the security and feasibility in this treatment, which was also testified by H&E staining slice observation from 1 to 7 weeks (Figs. S11 and S12). Moreover, five major organs (heart, kidneys, liver, lungs, and spleen) staining slices were listed in Fig. S13, suggesting the in vivo harmless and safety of GGBDFs during NIR anti-tumor therapy. Thus, GGBDFs could offer enhanced chemo-photothermal therapy performance due to their molecular targeting, pH-responsive DOX release, and high photothermal conversion efficiency.

In summary, we developed low toxicity, pH-responsive drug release, and targeted PTT by GdOF:Yb3+/Er3+@(GNDs@BSA)–DOX–FA nanohybrid for improved anti-tumor theranostics. Because of BSA coating and FA conjugating, these nanohybrids show low in vivo toxicity, favoring their drug delivery and biological circulation. With the pH value recognition profile of cis-aconityl linker, DOX was controlled to release within the acidic tumor microenvironment, reducing the normal cellular injury during the treatment. The cancer cell targeting ability of those nanohybrids could promote PTT induced cancer cell killing, by a large amount of heat generated from high photothermal conversion efficiency. Moreover, this nanoplatform exhibited multimodal imaging capabilities, which can monitor the tumor site in different approach. Such a strategy employing our novel nanohybrids is able to simultaneously obtain enhanced chemo-photothermal therapy and may be promising in exploiting multifunctional diagnosis of tumor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 51772059 and 51602072), the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (JJ2019ZZ0132), the Special Innovation Talents of Harbin Science and Technology (2016RAXXJ005), and the Fundamental Research funds for the Central Universities are greatly acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.03.219.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
